items.correct: When stressed (usually by warming), corals expel their symbiotic zooxanthellae (algae that provide colour and food), turning white. They can recover if stress eases, but die if it persists.
items.misconception: Coral bleaching is when coral is killed by sunlight bleaching its colour.
items.random_wrong: Coral bleaching is when boats spill chemicals that whiten the reef.
items.why: It's the loss of algal symbionts, not sun-fading. Bleached coral is not yet dead — it's a warning state. Repeated mass bleaching (like 2016 on the Great Barrier Reef) causes long-term reef collapse.

items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.

items.correct: The adaptation of global products or services to local tastes, regulations or cultures — e.g. McDonald's offering paneer burgers in India or Tesco selling local foods in different countries.
items.misconception: Glocalisation means the destruction of local cultures by global brands.
items.random_wrong: Glocalisation is a typo of globalisation.
items.why: TNCs glocalise to maximise local sales; it's a strategic response, not cultural homogenisation. Students sometimes describe globalisation effects as glocalisation, missing the active local-adaptation element.

items.correct: Long, narrow lake in glacial valley
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Long, narrow lake in glacial valley. “Round mountain lake” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: NW England. “Scotland” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many lakes are in the Lake District?
items.why: The real figure is 16. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
